channel.title: AI & Organization Management on Multi-Agent Future
channel.description: Real stories of building AI multi-agent systems — from automated trading bots to AI-managed organizations. No hype, just code and lessons.

item.title: How AI Is Flattening the Org Chart and Rewriting Middle Management
item.description:
The Prediction That Got Everyone’s Attention

In October 2024, Gartner dropped a prediction that traveled through every Slack channel and LinkedIn feed in corporate America: “Through 2026, 20% of organizations will use AI to flatten their organizational structure, eliminating more than half of current middle management positions.”

We’re now in March 2026. So where do we actually stand?

The short answer: the prediction wasn’t wrong, but the execution has been far messier than the clean narrative suggests. Some companies have aggressively cut middle management and seen real gains. Others did the same and are now quietly rehiring. The pattern is more interesting — and more instructive — than the headline.
item.content:encoded:
The Prediction That Got Everyone’s Attention

In October 2024, Gartner dropped a prediction that traveled through every Slack channel and LinkedIn feed in corporate America: “Through 2026, 20% of organizations will use AI to flatten their organizational structure, eliminating more than half of current middle management positions.”

We’re now in March 2026. So where do we actually stand?

The short answer: the prediction wasn’t wrong, but the execution has been far messier than the clean narrative suggests. Some companies have aggressively cut middle management and seen real gains. Others did the same and are now quietly rehiring. The pattern is more interesting — and more instructive — than the headline.

This post breaks down what’s actually happening on the ground, which roles are genuinely at risk, what the early movers got right and wrong, and what a realistic AI-augmented org structure looks like for companies that want to move fast without breaking their culture.

What Middle Managers Actually Do (And Which Parts AI Can Replace)

Before we talk about replacing middle management, we need to be honest about what they do. The caricature — the pointy-haired boss from Dilbert who adds no value — obscures a more complex reality.

Middle managers typically handle five categories of work:

1. Information routing and status reporting Collecting updates from teams, synthesizing them into dashboards or reports, and passing information up the chain. This is the most automatable function. AI agents already do this better than humans — they don’t forget, they don’t editorialize, and they work 24/7.

2. Task allocation and scheduling Deciding who works on what, managing calendars, resolving resource conflicts. Project management tools with AI capabilities (Asana’s AI features, Monday.com’s automations, Linear’s auto-assignment) have been chipping away at this for years. Agentic AI is finishing the job.

3. Performance monitoring Tracking KPIs, identifying underperformers, flagging risks. This is where AI genuinely excels — pattern recognition across large datasets, anomaly detection, and trend analysis. McKinsey’s 2025 State of AI survey found that redesigning workflows around AI had the single biggest impact on organizational performance across all 25 attributes they tested.

4. Decision-making within defined parameters Approving budgets under a threshold, deciding on routine escalations, choosing between predefined options. Rule-based decisions are exactly what AI handles well, especially when the decision criteria are documented.

5. People development, coaching, and culture Mentoring, resolving interpersonal conflicts, building team cohesion, navigating ambiguity, advocating for reports. This is where AI falls flat — and where the companies that over-automated are now paying the price.

The companies getting this right are automating categories 1-4 and reinvesting in category 5. The ones getting it wrong are treating all five categories as equivalent.

The Klarna Experiment: A Cautionary Tale in Three Acts

No company has been more publicly aggressive about AI-driven workforce reduction than Klarna, the Swedish buy-now-pay-later company. Their story is worth examining in detail because it contains every lesson about AI organizational transformation compressed into 18 months.

Act 1: The Bold Move (2024)

Klarna CEO Sebastian Siemiatkowski announced that the company’s AI assistant, powered by OpenAI, was doing the work of 700 full-time customer service agents. The company instituted a hiring freeze and let attrition do its work. By mid-2025, Klarna had shrunk from roughly 5,527 employees to around 2,907 — a 40% reduction. The AI was handling two-thirds of all customer service conversations.

The financial results looked spectacular. Revenue per employee skyrocketed. Marketing spend dropped. Siemiatkowski told the press that AI was handling the work of 853 full-time staff and the company was raising remaining employee salaries by 60%.

Act 2: The Cracks Appear (Late 2025)

Then the metrics that weren’t in the press releases started surfacing. Customer complaints increased. Satisfaction ratings dropped. The AI handled volume well but struggled with nuanced, complex, or emotionally charged interactions — exactly the ones that determine whether a customer stays or leaves. Reports emerged of generic, repetitive, and insufficiently nuanced automated responses.

The problem wasn’t the AI itself. The problem was that Klarna had removed the human judgment layer that knew when to escalate, how to read between the lines of a customer complaint, and when a policy exception would save a relationship worth more than the cost of the exception.

Act 3: The Reversal (2026)

Siemiatkowski eventually acknowledged the cuts had gone too far. Klarna started hiring again, specifically for roles that required human judgment and relationship management. A 2025 Orgvue survey found that 55% of companies that made employees redundant due to AI now regret those decisions.

The lesson isn’t that AI doesn’t work for organizational restructuring. It’s that treating AI deployment as a headcount reduction exercise instead of a role redesign exercise produces predictable failures.

Who’s Getting Organizational AI Right

Not every company followed the Klarna playbook. Some have been more thoughtful, and their results are more sustainable.

Salesforce: The “Digital Workforce” Model

Salesforce’s approach with Agentforce is instructive. Rather than replacing roles, they added a layer of AI agents that handle specific workflow segments — simulating product launches, orchestrating marketing campaigns, handling first-line customer queries. CEO Marc Benioff described it as creating a “digital workforce” where humans and agents work together.

The key distinction: Salesforce didn’t eliminate the product launch manager. They gave the product launch manager an AI team that handles the data collection, scheduling, stakeholder tracking, and status reporting so the human can focus on strategy, relationships, and the judgment calls that determine whether the launch succeeds.

What “AI High Performers” Do Differently

McKinsey’s data is revealing here. Their 2025 survey of nearly 2,000 organizations found that high-performing companies consistently differ from the rest across six dimensions: strategy, talent, operating model, technology, data, and adoption.

The critical finding: 32% of companies expect AI to reduce their total workforce by at least 3% within the next year, but AI high performers are more likely to predict both reductions and increases — they’re restructuring, not just cutting. They’re eliminating roles that are primarily information-routing while creating new roles focused on AI oversight, prompt engineering, and cross-functional coordination.

The companies that just cut headcount and declare victory are the ones that end up in the Orgvue regret statistics.

The New Org Chart: What Flatter Actually Looks Like

When Gartner talks about “flattening” organizational structure, what does that mean in practice? Based on early adopters, the pattern looks like this:

Before: The Traditional Pyramid

CEO
  └─ VP (Strategy, Budget)
       └─ Director (Department Goals, Resource Allocation)
            └─ Senior Manager (Team Oversight, Reporting)
                 └─ Manager (Task Assignment, Performance Tracking)
                      └─ Team Lead (Daily Coordination)
                           └─ Individual Contributors

Six layers between the person doing the work and the person setting the strategy.

After: The AI-Augmented Diamond

CEO + AI Strategy Dashboard
  └─ VP (Strategy + AI-Surfaced Insights)
       └─ Team Lead / Player-Coach (People + AI Agent Oversight)
            └─ Individual Contributors + AI Agents

Three to four layers. The Director, Senior Manager, and Manager layers collapse because:

  • Information routing → handled by AI dashboards and automated reports
  • Task allocation → handled by AI project management agents
  • Performance monitoring → handled by AI analytics with exception-based human review
  • Routine decisions → handled by AI within defined policy parameters
  • People development → stays human, elevated in importance

The remaining human manager role transforms into what some organizations call a “player-coach” — someone who both contributes individual work and handles the irreducibly human aspects of team leadership.

Duolingo: The AI-First Rewrite

Duolingo provides another data point. In January 2024, the language-learning company cut 10% of its contractor workforce, primarily content writers and translators, replacing them with AI. By mid-2025, CEO Luis von Ahn announced the company would phase out contractors entirely for work AI could handle, declaring Duolingo an “AI-first” company.

The organizational impact went beyond headcount. Duolingo’s content creation pipeline — which previously required human translators coordinated by project managers coordinated by content directors — collapsed into a system where a smaller team of AI trainers and quality reviewers worked directly with LLM-based generation tools.

The middle management layer that existed to coordinate human translators across 40+ languages became unnecessary when the coordination target shifted from humans to AI models.

This is the pattern that Gartner is tracking: it’s not that AI replaces the manager directly. It’s that AI replaces the work that required the manager to exist.

The Skills That Survive Organizational Flattening

If you’re a middle manager reading this and feeling uneasy, the productive question isn’t “will my job be eliminated?” It’s “which parts of my current role will still need a human in 18 months?”

Based on where early adopters are landing, these skills are appreciating in value:

1. Ambiguity Navigation

AI is excellent at optimizing within defined parameters. It’s terrible at deciding which parameters matter. When a project faces competing priorities with no clear framework for resolution — a client wants faster delivery but the team is burning out, or a cost cut will save money this quarter but damage a strategic relationship — that’s a human judgment call that requires understanding context, relationships, politics, and values simultaneously.

2. Cross-Functional Translation

As organizations flatten, the remaining human managers need to translate between domains more than ever. The engineering team, the sales team, and the finance team all have their own languages, priorities, and definitions of success. AI can surface data across these domains, but interpreting what that data means in context — and negotiating trade-offs that all parties can live with — requires human social intelligence.

3. Trust Building and Conflict Resolution

You cannot automate trust. When a team member is struggling, when two colleagues have a conflict that’s affecting output, when someone needs to hear hard feedback delivered with empathy — these moments define whether a team functions or fractures. Companies that eliminated these roles are learning that the cost of un-managed teams shows up in attrition, and attrition is expensive.

4. AI Oversight and Prompt Architecture

This is the genuinely new skill. Someone needs to design the prompts and workflows that AI agents follow, monitor their outputs for quality and bias, and know when to override automated decisions. This isn’t a junior role — it requires deep domain knowledge plus AI literacy. The best candidates are often experienced middle managers who understand the business context deeply enough to know when the AI is wrong.

5. Strategic Narrative and Change Communication

When organizations restructure — especially AI-driven restructuring that triggers job security anxiety — someone needs to explain why, paint a credible picture of the future, and maintain morale through the transition. This is leadership work that AI cannot do, and it’s the work that separates successful transformations from the 55% that end in regret.

A Practical Framework: The 70/20/10 Restructuring Model

For organizations considering AI-driven org flattening, here’s a framework based on what the successful early adopters are doing:

70% Augmentation

Take 70% of middle management tasks and augment them with AI. Don’t eliminate the role — transform it. The manager who spent 4 hours per day on status reports and task tracking now spends 30 minutes reviewing AI-generated reports and 3.5 hours on coaching, strategy, and cross-functional work.

20% Automation

Identify the 20% of roles that are genuinely and primarily information-routing. These are the roles where the person’s main contribution is collecting data from one group and presenting it to another. Automate these, but do it with a transition plan — retraining, redeployment, or generous severance.

10% Creation

Create new roles that didn’t exist before: AI operations managers, prompt engineers, human-AI workflow designers, AI ethics and quality reviewers. Hire from your existing middle management pool when possible — they have the domain knowledge that makes AI oversight effective.

Audit Prompt for Your Organization:

List every middle management role. For each one, categorize the 
time split across these five functions:

1. Information routing/reporting: ___% 
2. Task allocation/scheduling: ___%
3. Performance monitoring: ___%
4. Routine decision-making: ___%
5. People development/coaching/culture: ___%

Roles where #1-4 total > 80%: candidates for automation
Roles where #5 > 30%: candidates for augmentation and elevation
Roles where #1-4 total > 80% AND #5 < 10%: candidates for elimination

Run this audit before making any headcount decisions.

The Gartner Prediction, Revisited

So is Gartner’s prediction coming true? In March 2026, the answer is “partially, and not in the way most people expected.”

Yes, organizations are flattening. The McKinsey data confirms that workflow redesign is the single most impactful organizational change companies can make around AI. The IMD forecasts for 2026 describe this year as the shift from scaling AI pilots to full organizational overhaul.

But the 20% of organizations doing this successfully aren’t eliminating middle management wholesale. They’re decomposing the role into its component functions, automating the mechanical parts, and elevating the human parts. The organizations that treated this as a cost-cutting exercise are the ones now dealing with regret, rehiring, and the morale damage that comes from employees watching their colleagues get replaced by chatbots.

The real prediction should have been: “Through 2026, 20% of organizations will fundamentally redesign middle management roles around AI capabilities, and the ones that confuse redesign with elimination will learn an expensive lesson about what humans actually do.”

What to Do This Quarter

Whether you’re a CEO planning a restructure or a middle manager planning your career, here are concrete next steps:

If you lead an organization:

  • Run the audit prompt above before making any structural changes
  • Start with augmentation pilots, not elimination programs
  • Measure what happens to team satisfaction and retention when you automate management tasks — not just what happens to cost
  • Talk to Klarna’s competitors about what happened to their customer satisfaction during the aggressive-cut phase

If you’re a middle manager:

  • Audit your own time. Honestly categorize where your hours go across the five functions above
  • If more than 70% of your time is information routing and task tracking, you’re in the automation zone — start shifting toward coaching, strategy, and cross-functional work now
  • Learn AI tools deeply enough to oversee them, not just use them
  • Position yourself as the person who knows the domain well enough to tell the AI when it’s wrong

If you’re building AI agent systems:

  • Design for augmentation first, replacement second
  • Build in human escalation paths that are easy to use, not friction-heavy “are you sure?” dialogues
  • Instrument your systems to measure quality degradation, not just volume metrics
  • Remember that the Klarna lesson applies to your customers too

What’s Next in This Series

Next week, we’ll look at how AI is reshaping performance reviews and feedback cycles — another middle management staple that’s being fundamentally rethought. We’ll examine companies that have replaced annual reviews with AI-powered continuous feedback systems and whether the data supports the hype.

This post is part of the AI & Organization Management series, exploring how artificial intelligence is transforming organizational structure, leadership, and team dynamics with real case studies and actionable frameworks.

item.title: AI Is Killing the Annual Performance Review (Finally)
item.description:
The Annual Review Is a Ritual Everyone Hates

Here’s a number that should embarrass every HR department in America: 90% of managers are dissatisfied with their own annual review process. Not 90% of employees — 90% of the people running the reviews.

The annual performance review is one of those corporate traditions that persists not because it works, but because nobody has found a replacement that satisfies legal, HR compliance teams, and executive dashboards simultaneously. It’s the organizational equivalent of the fax machine — everyone knows it’s broken, most workplaces still have one.
item.content:encoded:
The Annual Review Is a Ritual Everyone Hates

Here’s a number that should embarrass every HR department in America: 90% of managers are dissatisfied with their own annual review process. Not 90% of employees — 90% of the people running the reviews.

The annual performance review is one of those corporate traditions that persists not because it works, but because nobody has found a replacement that satisfies legal, HR compliance teams, and executive dashboards simultaneously. It’s the organizational equivalent of the fax machine — everyone knows it’s broken, most workplaces still have one.

The evidence has been piling up for a decade. One-third of U.S. companies have been actively moving away from traditional appraisals since 2016. Deloitte, Accenture, Adobe, and GE all publicly ditched their annual review processes. But for the remaining two-thirds, inertia won.

Until now. AI-powered performance management tools are doing something the previous wave of “continuous feedback” platforms never quite pulled off: they’re making the alternative less work, not more. And that changes the calculus entirely.

Why Previous Attempts to Kill the Annual Review Failed

Before we get to the AI angle, it’s worth understanding why the “death of the annual review” has been predicted roughly once per year since 2014, and why it never quite stuck.

The problem wasn’t the diagnosis. Everyone agreed that a single annual conversation was insufficient to drive performance. The problem was the prescription.

Continuous feedback requires continuous effort. When companies like Adobe replaced annual reviews with “Check-Ins,” they were asking managers to have frequent, structured conversations with each direct report. In theory, great. In practice, you’re asking the busiest people in the organization to do more of the thing they already don’t have time for.

Weekly check-in tools created notification fatigue. Platforms like 15Five built their entire product around weekly manager-employee check-ins. The early adoption curves looked promising. The 12-month retention curves did not. Managers started treating the weekly prompts the same way most of us treat the “how was your experience?” emails from airlines — ignored until absolutely forced.

HR lost its audit trail. Annual reviews, for all their flaws, produce a document. That document is discoverable in lawsuits, useful for promotion justification, and required by many compliance frameworks. When you replace one annual document with 52 weekly conversations, the question becomes: who’s writing all that down? The answer, usually, was nobody.

Calibration became impossible. One of the hidden functions of annual review cycles is calibration — the process where managers compare ratings across teams to ensure consistency. Hard to calibrate when Team A does monthly check-ins, Team B does quarterly reviews, and Team C’s manager forgot to log anything since August.

So the annual review persisted, not because anyone loved it, but because the alternatives created new problems without fully solving the old ones.

What’s Different About AI-Powered Performance Management

The current generation of AI performance tools isn’t just adding a chatbot to the old process. It’s restructuring how performance data gets captured, synthesized, and acted on. The shift is architectural, not cosmetic.

Passive Data Collection Replaces Active Documentation

The fundamental breakthrough is this: instead of asking humans to document performance continuously, AI systems observe work artifacts that already exist.

Windmill, one of the more aggressive players in this space, integrates with Slack, GitHub, Jira, and calendar tools to build a continuous picture of what each employee is actually doing. When review time comes, the system drafts a summary based on months of observed activity — commits merged, projects shipped, meetings led, peer interactions logged.

Culture Amp takes a different approach, connecting employee engagement survey data to performance patterns. Their AI identifies correlations that human managers miss: the team whose engagement scores dropped three months before their delivery metrics declined, or the individual whose peer recognition frequency predicts their likelihood of promotion.

The critical distinction from previous continuous feedback tools: the AI does the documentation work. Managers review and edit a draft rather than starting from a blank page. According to Lattice, managers currently spend one to two weeks completing a single review cycle. AI-assisted reviews cut that to hours.

Continuous Signal, Periodic Synthesis

The smarter implementations have landed on a hybrid model that I think will become standard: AI captures signals continuously, but humans still make decisions at defined intervals.

This matters because it addresses the HR audit trail problem. You get a documented review at whatever cadence your legal team requires — quarterly, semi-annually, annually — but that document is built from months of continuous observation rather than two weeks of panicked recollection.

Betterworks and Leapsome both use this approach. Their AI systems generate what amounts to a “first draft” performance review, pre-populated with specific examples, goal progress data, and peer feedback summaries. The manager’s job becomes editorial: confirm, adjust, add context, have the conversation.

Calibration at Scale

This might be the most underappreciated capability. AI can compare language, rating patterns, and evaluation criteria across hundreds of reviews simultaneously — something that calibration committees attempt to do in a conference room over three hours with rapidly declining attention spans.

PerformYard specifically markets bias detection in review language. Their system flags reviews that use different adjective patterns for different demographic groups — a known problem in manual reviews where, for example, women are more likely to receive feedback about communication style while men receive feedback about technical outcomes.

The Lattice Cautionary Tale

No discussion of AI in performance management is complete without the Lattice story, because it illustrates exactly where the boundary sits between helpful augmentation and organizational overcorrection.

In July 2024, Lattice announced they would be the first platform to give AI “digital workers” official employee records — onboarding them, training them, assigning goals, performance metrics, and even managers. Just like human employees.

The backlash was immediate and fierce. HR professionals and tech workers pushed back hard. Within days, Lattice reversed course, posting that they “will not further pursue digital workers in the product.”

Then, in March 2025, Lattice CEO Sarah Franklin told Forbes that AI agents still need to be rated on their performance — just as human workers are. The framing had evolved from “AI as employee” to “AI as tool that needs evaluation,” but the underlying tension remained.

The lesson here is crucial for any organization implementing AI in performance management: the technology works best when it augments human judgment, not when it replaces human identity. Employees don’t mind AI helping their manager write a better review. They very much mind being treated as equivalent to a chatbot.

The Legal Minefield Nobody’s Talking About

If you’re implementing AI-assisted performance reviews, your legal team needs to be in the room. Here’s why.

Automated Decision Systems and Employment Law

The HR Defense Blog reported that multiple jurisdictions now classify AI performance tools as “automated-decision systems” (ADS), making it unlawful to use any ADS that discriminates against employees based on protected traits in personnel decisions.

New York City’s Local Law 144 already requires annual bias audits for automated employment decision tools. Illinois, Colorado, and the EU (under the AI Act) have similar or stricter requirements taking effect in 2025 and 2026.

The practical implication: if your AI performance tool surfaces a recommendation — “this employee is underperforming based on our analysis” — and that recommendation disproportionately affects a protected class, your company is exposed. CPO Magazine’s 2026 legal forecast warns that using AI algorithms without bias audits can lead to class-action exposure under Title VII and ADEA.

The Bias Problem Is Real, Not Hypothetical

A 2025 University of Melbourne study found that AI-powered hiring tools struggled to accurately evaluate candidates with speech disabilities or heavy non-native accents. The same pattern applies to performance evaluation: if your AI is analyzing Slack messages, it will evaluate differently based on writing style, English proficiency, and communication frequency — all of which correlate with protected characteristics.

This doesn’t mean you shouldn’t use AI in performance management. It means you need:

  1. Regular bias audits by an independent third party
  2. Transparency about what data the AI uses and how
  3. Human override on every consequential decision (termination, promotion, compensation)
  4. Documentation that the AI’s role is advisory, not determinative

How to Actually Implement This (Without Getting Fired or Sued)

Here’s the practical playbook, based on what I’ve seen working at companies that are doing this well.

Phase 1: Instrument Before You Automate (Weeks 1-4)

Before you buy any AI performance tool, you need to understand what performance data you already generate.

Audit your existing tool stack. Map every system where work artifacts live: project management (Jira, Asana, Linear), communication (Slack, Teams), code (GitHub, GitLab), documents (Google Workspace, Notion), CRM (Salesforce, HubSpot). This becomes your AI’s observation surface.

Identify your review cadence requirements. Talk to legal and compliance. What documentation do they need, and how often? This determines whether you’re building a quarterly, semi-annual, or annual synthesis cycle.

Benchmark your current process cost. Measure how many hours managers spend on reviews today. You’ll need this number to justify the investment and to measure whether the AI actually reduced the burden.

EXECUTION PROMPT — Tool Stack Audit

"List every tool where our team creates work artifacts (code commits, 
project updates, messages, documents, meeting notes). For each tool, 
identify: (1) Does it have an API? (2) What performance-relevant data 
does it contain? (3) Who owns the admin access? Create a spreadsheet 
with columns: Tool | Data Type | API Available | Admin Owner | 
Integration Priority (High/Medium/Low)"

Phase 2: Pick Your Platform (Weeks 4-6)

The market is crowded. Here’s how the major players differentiate:

Platform Best For AI Capability Price Range
Windmill Engineering teams Slack-first, auto-drafts from work data Mid-range
Culture Amp Enterprise, engagement-focused Connects surveys to performance Premium
Lattice General purpose, mid-market Goal tracking + AI review drafts Mid-range
Leapsome European companies (GDPR) Multi-source AI synthesis Mid-range
Betterworks Large enterprise OKR alignment + AI calibration Premium
PerformYard Bias-conscious orgs Language analysis + demographic flagging Budget-friendly

My honest recommendation: if you’re under 200 employees, start with Leapsome or PerformYard. If you’re engineering-heavy, look at Windmill. If you’re enterprise with an existing Culture Amp engagement deployment, extend it into performance. Don’t buy a platform just because it has the flashiest AI demo.

Phase 3: Run a Parallel Cycle (Months 2-4)

Do not — I repeat, do not — replace your existing review process on day one. Run the AI system in parallel for at least one full review cycle.

This means managers complete their normal reviews AND receive the AI-generated draft. Then you compare:

  • Accuracy: Did the AI capture the same themes the manager identified?
  • Completeness: Did the AI surface things the manager missed (or vice versa)?
  • Bias: Did the AI’s language patterns differ across demographic groups?
  • Time savings: How long did the AI-assisted version take vs. the traditional version?
EXECUTION PROMPT — Parallel Review Analysis

"Compare the AI-generated review draft with the manager-written review 
for each employee. Score on: (1) Theme alignment (1-5), (2) Specific 
example quality (1-5), (3) Actionable feedback presence (Y/N), 
(4) Any concerning language patterns. Summarize findings across all 
reviews in an executive brief with a go/no-go recommendation for 
full deployment."

Phase 4: Transition With Manager Training (Months 4-6)

The biggest failure mode isn’t the technology. It’s managers who either rubber-stamp the AI output without adding judgment, or managers who ignore the AI output entirely and revert to their old habits.

You need structured training that covers:

  1. How to edit an AI draft — where to add personal context, specific examples, and forward-looking development goals that the AI can’t observe
  2. When to override — recognizing when the AI’s data picture is incomplete (parental leave, internal transfer, project scope change)
  3. Legal guardrails — what language to avoid, how to document that the final review reflects human judgment
  4. The conversation itself — AI can write the document, but the human delivers it face-to-face

Phase 5: Measure and Iterate (Ongoing)

Track three metrics quarterly:

  1. Manager time per review cycle — this should drop 40-60% in the first year
  2. Employee satisfaction with feedback quality — survey this; it should improve
  3. Bias audit results — run these quarterly with the vendor or an independent firm

If manager time isn’t dropping, the tool isn’t integrated properly. If employee satisfaction isn’t improving, the managers are either rubber-stamping or the AI’s quality is poor. If bias audits show problems, pause and fix before continuing.

What This Looks Like in Two Years

Here’s my prediction for where AI performance management lands by 2028:

The annual review becomes a quarterly synthesis. The annual cadence dies not with a dramatic announcement but with a gradual compression. Companies move to quarterly AI-generated summaries with annual compensation decisions. The “review” becomes a conversation about a document that largely wrote itself.

Manager quality becomes more visible, not less. When the AI handles documentation, what’s left for the manager is pure leadership: coaching, context-setting, career development, difficult conversations. The managers who were hiding behind the paperwork get exposed. The managers who were good at the human parts get more time to do them.

Performance data becomes bidirectional. Current tools evaluate employees. Next-generation tools will evaluate managers too — analyzing how their feedback patterns, response times, and coaching quality correlate with team outcomes. Gallup’s data showing that only 21% of employees feel they control their own performance metrics will start to shift as AI makes the evaluation process more transparent and data-driven.

Regulatory frameworks catch up. The patchwork of NYC, Illinois, Colorado, and EU regulations will consolidate into something more coherent. Companies that got ahead of bias audits and transparency requirements will have a significant advantage over those that didn’t.

The Bottom Line

The annual performance review has survived for decades not because it works, but because the alternatives were more work for everyone involved. AI changes that equation by handling the documentation burden — the part humans are worst at — while leaving the judgment, coaching, and human connection to the people who should be doing it.

58% of companies still track performance in spreadsheets. If that’s you, you’re not just behind on technology. You’re building legal exposure, burning manager time, and producing reviews that neither managers nor employees trust.

The path forward isn’t replacing human judgment with AI. It’s freeing human judgment from busywork so it can actually matter.

This is Part 2 of the AI & Organization Management series. Part 1 covered how AI is flattening the org chart and rewriting middle management. Next up: AI-powered hiring — what actually works, what’s hype, and the legal framework you need before you deploy any of it.
